video_title: You’re About to See the Real AI Winners Stand Up
gist: American Century Investments CEO Jonathan Thomas joins The Compound and Friends to explain how the asset-management industry's client relationship has shifted from asset manager to advisor to platform, how Avantis grew to $150B AUM in 6.5 years on low-cost factor ETFs, and why he sees AI winners rotating from 'creators' (Nvidia) to 'adopters' across every sector, with a genuine but underappreciated 'earnings bubble' risk if circular AI/Neocloud financing unwinds.

insights.claim: American Century Investments CEO Jonathan Thomas frames the industry's client relationship as having moved through three eras: asset-manager-owned in the 1980s-90s, advisor-owned after the 2000 tech bust, and now an early-stage shift toward platforms (retirement platforms, AI-assisted financial assistants) owning the relationship, which he expects to 'end poorly' for client trust and behavioral coaching.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Asset managers and advisors should watch platform disintermediation risk; distribution-owning platforms could erode the trust/behavioral-coaching value advisors provide during downturns, the way ETFs already squeezed active mutual funds.
insights.quote: I think it probably ends poorly because that trust that's such so important in our business

insights.claim: Thomas says that 20-30 years ago roughly 75-80% of asset managers had positive net investment flows, but today that has completely flipped to 75-80% having negative flows, a structural reversal for the industry.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Broad structural headwind for legacy active managers implies continued industry consolidation and fee pressure; differentiated, low-cost product lines (like Avantis) are increasingly necessary to buck the trend.
insights.quote: 20 30 years ago 80% 75% of the asset managers had positive net investment. Today it's totally flipped. It's about 75% 80% or negative.

insights.claim: Thomas attributes BlackRock's reported 20% year-over-year AUM growth partly to a five-year market tailwind (stocks up roughly 16% annualized on average) plus a technology-driven consolidation dynamic where scale winners in asset management keep taking disproportionate share, even as smaller brands like Avantis can still 'catch fire.'
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Scale advantages in asset management appear to be compounding, so smaller managers need sharply differentiated, low-cost products to win flows rather than competing head-on with mega-managers on breadth.
insights.quote: there are brands like Avantis that come along

themes.theme: Avantis and the Active-ETF Land Grab

insights.claim: Avantis's first five products with five-year track records carry an average expense ratio of 26 basis points (versus 19 bps for their passive category equivalents) but have outperformed their benchmarks by an average of 280 basis points annually, and the suite reached $150 billion in AUM within about 6.5 years of its 2019 launch.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: A concrete value-for-fee data point: paying roughly 7 bps more than a passive index fund bought Avantis investors nearly 3 percentage points of annual outperformance, a benchmark advisors can use when evaluating active-ETF alternatives to Vanguard/BlackRock indexing.
insights.quote: on average, we've outperformed the bench by 280 bips

insights.claim: Avantis applies the same factor-based, academically-driven ('financial science') investing philosophy consistently across every asset class (large-cap growth, small-cap value, emerging markets, etc.), which Thomas says drives advisors who buy one Avantis fund to typically buy three or four positions once they understand the philosophy.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: A consistent cross-asset-class philosophy functions as a cross-sell moat, increasing share of an advisor's book per relationship; other multi-product managers could replicate this to improve stickiness.

insights.claim: Active ETFs made up roughly 40% of year-to-date ETF flows as of mid-2026, the category has grown over 50% in five years, and American Century's own ETF business went from about $50 billion and 1-2% market share when it entered in 2018 to nearly $2 trillion and 11% market share industry-wide today.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Structural, still-growing shift of flows into active ETFs versus mutual funds; positions issuers with early, credible active-ETF franchises to keep gaining share even as the overall active-vs-passive debate rages on.
insights.quote: roughly, uh, correct, 40% of the yeartoate ETF flows have gone into active ETFs

insights.claim: The SEC's 2019 'ETF Rule' (Rule 6c-11) removed the requirement for issuers to obtain one-by-one exemptive relief to launch active ETFs, a regulatory unlock that Thomas calls a major tailwind for the active-ETF boom.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: A specific regulatory catalyst (2019 rule change) underpins the active-ETF growth thesis; investors tracking the category's runway should note the removal of this friction as a structural, not cyclical, driver.

insights.claim: Contrary to industry fears that daily portfolio-holdings transparency would let others front-run active managers' trades, Thomas says full transparency in active ETFs has caused no discernible harm ('the world didn't come to an end'), which discredits the earlier justification for semi-transparent ETF structures.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: The front-running objection to transparent active ETFs appears empirically debunked, which should accelerate remaining holdouts converting mutual funds to fully transparent ETF structures.
insights.quote: we started with that and one of my management philosophies is you got to take risk

insights.claim: American Century's own first attempt at active ETFs used a semi-transparent structure, meant to protect portfolio manager secrecy from front-running, but it got no investor uptake and was scrapped in favor of a fully transparent structure that succeeded.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Case study confirming semi-transparent ETF wrappers were a dead-end product design; issuers still using them should expect continued lack of adoption relative to fully transparent structures.

insights.claim: Thomas expects mutual-fund-to-ETF cannibalization to keep accelerating because future advisor flows increasingly go into all-ETF model portfolios; firms that delay converting 40 Act funds into ETF share classes risk losing all of that future advisor-directed growth, and he says American Century has moved from roughly the 20th-largest overall asset manager to the 4th-largest active ETF issuer and top-10/top-5 overall in ETFs.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: The shift to ETF wrappers is reshuffling the asset-manager hierarchy into a 'jump ball' where nimble issuers that moved early can leapfrog legacy giants — a signal for which fund families are structurally gaining versus losing share.
insights.quote: we're now the fourth largest active ETF issuer in the country

insights.claim: Thomas credits Cathie Wood's ARK Invest with pioneering the mainstream active-ETF category by building a roughly $50 billion actively managed ETF shop during the pandemic-era momentum/growth-stock run, which he says opened the door for other asset managers to launch active ETFs even though ARK's own performance struggled in subsequent years.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Credits an unlikely, later-struggling product (ARK) with de-risking active-ETF adoption industry-wide — useful context for separating pioneers that created durable structural change from those whose performance later faded.
insights.quote: she was the first one to have a $50 billion aum actively managed ETF shop

insights.claim: Because actively managed mutual funds often must close once they get too large to preserve alpha (disrupting advisors who hold them in models), American Century designed its ETFs — including small-cap strategies — to hold enough underlying names to scale perpetually without closing.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Diversified, rules-based ETF strategies can absorb far more AUM growth than concentrated stock-picking funds, a durability factor to weigh when assessing capacity risk in actively managed products.

themes.theme: Market Breadth and the 'Earnings Bubble' Debate

insights.claim: Analysts were expecting 23.2% year-over-year S&P 500 earnings growth for the quarter being reported, with growth still at 20.9% excluding the Magnificent Seven, roughly 65% growth in the technology sector, and 10 of 11 S&P sectors growing earnings.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Earnings growth is broad-based across sectors, not confined to mega-cap tech, which undercuts the narrative that this rally rests narrowly on a handful of AI names.
insights.quote: excluding the mag 7 earnings are still expected to grow 20.9%

insights.claim: Thomas points out that for the first time in years, over 50% of S&P 500 constituents are up more than 10% year-to-date, and the non-Mag-7 '493' stocks are outperforming the Magnificent Seven so far in 2026, evidence of genuine market broadening.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: The rotation from AI 'creators' to AI 'adopters' is already showing up in realized stock returns, supporting a case for broadening portfolio exposure beyond mega-cap tech.
insights.quote: for the first time in I don't know how long 50 some odd percent of the S&P is up over 10%

insights.claim: Thomas notes the S&P 500 has hit 24 new all-time highs year-to-date in 2026 versus 57 in each of the prior two years and zero in 2022 and 2023, and argues that historically clusters of new highs are more indicative of an ongoing bull market than an imminent pullback.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Counters investor anxiety about buying at all-time highs; the historical pattern argues that frequent new highs have preceded continuation rather than reversal, relevant for market-timing decisions.
insights.quote: it's more indicative of an ongoing bull market than it is sort of any sort of an imminent recession or or pullback

insights.claim: Bears have pivoted their bubble argument: instead of claiming AI mega-cap stocks are overvalued (multiples are actually compressing), the new claim is that current profitability/earnings are unsustainably elevated and will fall once AI capex spending normalizes — the 'earnings bubble' thesis that Thomas calls a reasonable concern.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI bubble debate from a valuation-multiple risk to an earnings-durability risk; investors should stress-test AI-related earnings for sensitivity to a capex slowdown rather than relying only on P/E multiples as a bubble signal.
insights.quote: So that's where the earnings bubble comes from. The companies are over earning relative to what happens when this capex normalizes.

insights.claim: Nvidia trades at roughly 18x forward earnings, below the S&P 500's multiple, despite no deceleration in its growth or earnings guidance, which Thomas and the host interpret as the market already discounting future competition from ASICs, hyperscaler custom chips, and more GPU-efficient large language models rather than signaling a bubble.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Nvidia's multiple compression despite strong fundamentals is a valuation anomaly — not a bubble signal — that investors may want to explore as a mispricing rather than avoid out of bubble fear.
insights.quote: this would be a bubble if Nvidia's multiple had gone from 30 to 90 times earnings didn't happen this is a discount to the market

insights.claim: The host describes a 'circular AI financing' risk: sovereign-wealth- and private-equity-backed Neoclouds (citing Saudi Arabia, Norway, and Apollo as examples) commit to buying GPUs from Nvidia before they have the capital, sometimes with Nvidia itself backstopping the purchase, so Nvidia books revenue from sales financed by an interlocking chain of commitments that could unravel if any counterparty's funding falls through; Thomas confirms this is the real mechanism behind the earnings-bubble risk.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: This vendor-financing/backstop chain is the concrete mechanism that could turn an 'earnings bubble' into a real earnings collapse; investors should monitor Nvidia's financing exposure to Neocloud counterparties and their credit quality as a systemic risk gauge for AI infrastructure spending.
insights.quote: everybody's lending andor equity investing in everyone else's projects and the concern is when the music stops all these people go for the chairs

insights.claim: Thomas uses Jensen Huang's 'AI cake' framework of five layers — power/cooling infrastructure, chips, data-center infrastructure, models/LLMs, and applications — noting capital intensity is highest at the bottom layers (power, chips) and declines moving up, and argues investors must identify which layer a company occupies and whether that position is sustainable, expandable, and defensible.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: A concrete stock-picking framework for AI exposure: map each holding to a specific layer of the AI stack and assess defensibility there, rather than treating 'AI stock' as a monolithic category.
insights.quote: you got to figure out which level of the cake you're in

insights.claim: AI adoption benefits are broadening well beyond technology into industrials, utilities, and energy — Thomas cites Caterpillar as an unexpected 'AI story' — and he flags healthcare and financials (insurance underwriting, drug discovery) as sectors that could be among the biggest future beneficiaries as AI adopters rather than AI creators.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: A non-obvious sector-rotation idea: investors under-exposed to AI-adoption beneficiaries outside tech (industrials via power/data-center buildout, healthcare/financials via productivity gains) may be missing the next leg of the AI trade.
insights.quote: we wrote about Caterpillar and that's been such an AI story

insights.claim: Thomas predicts that if AI adopters realize the productivity benefits he expects (which drive margins, profits, and GDP), AI-related earnings growth will 'tick back up,' potentially allowing the broader S&P 493 to out-earn the Magnificent Seven and unwind market concentration without requiring a 2022-style bear market.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Offers a bullish scenario in which market-concentration risk resolves via broad earnings catch-up rather than a crash in mega-cap AI leaders; productivity and margin data are the key indicators to watch for this thesis playing out.
insights.quote: If the adopters realize the expected benefit productivity because productivity is what drives margin, profits, GDP, the economy, everything.

themes.theme: Demographics, Labor, and the AI Productivity Bet

insights.claim: Referencing a viral 'Centrini report' that got about 11 million views in a weekend and coincided with a market selloff around the start of the Iran war, Thomas argues the report's labor-market-disruption warnings ignored that the world may be at 'peak population,' with declining birth rates in Korea, China, and Japan and eventually the US, meaning AI-driven productivity gains could offset a shrinking workforce rather than simply displacing a growing one.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: A macro counter-narrative to AI-driven unemployment fears: declining global population growth could mean AI substitutes for a shrinking labor pool rather than displacing a growing one — worth tracking labor-force participation and immigration trends alongside AI capex data.
insights.quote: if we're at peak population and it continues to go down and productivity goes up, you're threading a needle, but it could create the perfect outcome

insights.claim: Despite his broadly non-negative long-run view on AI and labor, Thomas expects a possible 'air pocket' in white-collar employment over the next five years, even as he sees rising demand for robotics and AI-assisted caregiving to support roughly 68 million living baby boomers who will need physical care.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Distinguishes a near-term white-collar disruption risk (five-year horizon) from a long-run demand thesis for robotics/caregiving automation tied to an aging population — two separate AI-labor investment angles to track.
insights.quote: we might have a little air pocket in the next 5 years for white collar employment

themes.theme: Purpose-Driven Ownership: American Century and the Stowers Institute

insights.claim: American Century Investments directs over 40% of its annual dividends (roughly $2.2-2.3 billion cumulatively) to the Stowers Institute for Medical Research, a structure Thomas says he uses as a closing argument and frequent 'tiebreaker' when competing against larger rivals in institutional finals.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: A concrete example of how a non-financial differentiator (ownership/purpose structure) functions as a real institutional-sales edge for a mid-sized manager competing against BlackRock, Vanguard, and Fidelity on scale.
insights.quote: know that 40% of the dividends that we generate will go to fund medical research to create a better world for you, your kids

insights.claim: Thomas argues American Century's private, non-public ownership removes quarterly earnings pressure, letting the firm invest and reinvest for the long term in ways he believes publicly traded competitors cannot match.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: An ownership-structure thesis: privately held asset managers may have a durable capital-allocation advantage over public peers in funding long-payoff bets such as new product lines or research, relevant when assessing competitive dynamics among asset managers.

insights.claim: A Stowers Institute discovery — studying a fish species' ability to regenerate bone — was licensed to Amgen, which now sells a drug that can reverse, not just slow, osteoporosis, one of the rare instances where the nonprofit research arm's foundational science was directly commercialized.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Thomas
insights.so_what: Illustrates a tangible pipeline from nonprofit foundational research to commercial pharma outcomes, reinforcing the credibility of American Century's purpose-driven differentiation story with a real monetization event.
insights.quote: they have a drug that not only slows but can reverse osteoporosis
